sourceText: Yasawi overcame this by pioneering the use of the vernacular Turkic language for religious poetry. His 'Diwan-i-Hikmet' (Book of Wisdom) used familiar poetic forms and simple language to convey the core tenets of Sufi love and ethics, making them accessible and resonant for a vast audience.
He who is without love is without faith.
This emphasizes that the core of true faith lies in a deep and sincere love for God and all of creation. Love is the foundation of the spiritual journey.
Listen to the words of the wise and act upon them; flee from the ignorant and go far away.
This teaches the importance of seeking guidance from wise teachers for spiritual growth and avoiding ignorant, worldly influences that darken the soul.
Those who love the world cannot say they truly love God.
This indicates the incompatibility of attachment to the transient world with devotion to the eternal God. The true spiritual path requires transcending worldly desires.

Originally the heir to the throne, she was sidelined by the birth of her younger brother. After her father's death, her plot to overthrow her brother and install her husband as emperor failed, leading to her loss of everything and confinement in a monastery.
During her exile in the monastery, she transformed her grief and anger into scholarship. By writing 'The Alexiad,' a history of her father's reign, she immortalized herself as a historian.
For one purpose, and one only, do I write this, to solace my own grief.
In the preface to the 'Alexiad,' she frankly states that her motivation for writing history was to overcome the personal sorrow and pain of her political defeat.
For history, I think, is a kind of safeguard, and a treasury of the deeds of the past.
She viewed history as a vital means of preserving and evaluating the deeds of the past, intending to provide lessons for future generations.
